step.title: Scope Checkpoint
step.goal: Present the agent's understanding back to the user and obtain explicit confirmation, redirection, or rejection BEFORE scaffolding the bundle or entering Q↔R. This is the first firm checkpoint of the ticket-writing workflow. Skipping it risks burning research time on the wrong problem.

substep.title: Present Restatement + Candidate Tag

check.action: Lock {{session.request_restatement}}, {{session.candidate_tag}}, {{session.slug}} as authoritative. Proceed to step 6.

Capture the user's correction verbatim. Update {{session.request_restatement}} to reflect the redirected scope. Re-check the candidate tag against the new scope (it may have changed). Re-derive the slug if the scope shift makes the old slug misleading. Loop back to substep 5a to confirm the corrected version.

Update {{session.candidate_tag}} to the user-specified value. Validate it is one of the six primary tags (feature, bug, exploration, hotfix, infrastructure, chore). If not, push back and re-ask. Proceed to step 6.
Update {{session.slug}} to the user-specified value. Validate kebab-case format. Proceed to step 6.
Halt the workflow. No bundle has been created on disk; no cleanup is needed. Acknowledge the cancellation and return control to the ticketsmith menu.

substep.title: Display Locked Scope

section.title: Why firm, not soft
section.description: Spec §3.3 lists this as a firm checkpoint. Soft transitions (autonomous proceed) are reserved for situations where the agent cannot meaningfully misread the user's intent. Scope misread at step 5 cascades into wrong Q↔R turns, wrong subagent dispatch, wrong design alignment — recoverable but expensive. Firm checkpoint here saves session-hours later.

rules.rule: Do not proceed past 5b without a confirm/adjust signal. Silence is not consent.
If YOLO mode is active, this step still applies — critical="true" overrides YOLO per workflow engine rules.

section.title: Redirect handling
section.description: Redirects are normal. The user may have phrased the original request imprecisely; the restatement surfaces the gap. When redirected: - Treat the redirection as authoritative - Do not push back unless the redirect conflicts with the six-tag taxonomy or asks for something out of scope (e.g., "write the plan too") - Loop back to re-present; do not assume the corrected scope is self-evidently right

rules.rule: If user asks for a primary tag not in the six-tag taxonomy: explain the taxonomy and ask which fits

If user asks to skip Q↔R or jump straight to writing: refuse with reasoning. The Q↔R phase is non-negotiable for tags feature/exploration/infrastructure; bug/hotfix/chore have lighter Q↔R but still mandatory
If user asks ticket-orient to do plan-writing or implementation: refuse; those are out of scope per spec §13
